beats.text: Value methods fit a value function and read a policy off it; policy gradients optimize the policy itself.

visual.sub: No argmax. The policy is the answer.

beats.text: Write the policy as a differentiable function and do gradient ascent on the expected discounted return.

visual.note: EQ R4.1 — the objective, viewed as a function of theta

beats.text: Continuous actions are the first reason: a network outputs the action directly instead of searching for an argmax.

visual.label: joints a robot pose can have

beats.text: Stochastic optimal policies are the second reason; rock-paper-scissors has no good deterministic strategy.
Smooth improvement is the third: a small parameter change is a small policy change, not a sudden flip.
The cost of directness is that gradient estimates are unbiased but high variance, the chapter's central theme.
To ascend the objective we need its gradient, but theta sits inside the distribution we average over.
The log-derivative trick turns the gradient of an expectation into an expectation of a gradient.

visual.note: EQ R4.2 — the single identity behind every policy gradient

beats.text: Applied to trajectories, environment transitions differentiate to zero and only the policy terms survive.

visual.note: EQ R4.3 — score times a scalar weight Psi

beats.text: Each step raises the log-probability of actions that led to high reward and lowers it for low reward.
For a softmax policy at probability 0.6, the score for the sampled action is one minus that probability.
The score estimator is unbiased but high variance, since one return multiplies every action's score.
REINFORCE is the simplest realization: run an episode, weight each score by the return-to-go.
Only rewards after an action enter its return-to-go, the causality refinement that already cuts variance.

visual.note: EQ R4.4 — REINFORCE update, G_t is the return-to-go

beats.text: If every return hovers near plus one hundred, all actions get reinforced and the shared offset dominates.

visual.label: shared reward offset drowning the signal

beats.text: Subtracting any action-independent baseline leaves the gradient unbiased because the expected score is zero.

visual.note: EQ R4.5 — the baseline adds zero in expectation

beats.text: Re-centering returns around their typical value keeps the gradient direction but sharply lowers its variance.
The best baseline is the state value, turning the weight into the advantage and pointing straight at actor-critic.

visual.note: did this action beat the policy's own average from here?

beats.text: Actor-critic learns the baseline as its own function, then uses it to bootstrap instead of waiting for the full return.
The actor is the policy that chooses actions; the critic is a value function that judges how good a state is.
The TD error compares a better-informed value estimate against the critic's current prediction.

visual.note: EQ R4.6 — a low-variance, one-sample advantage estimate

beats.text: The same delta drives both updates: it pushes the actor and tells the critic how wrong its estimate was.

visual.note: EQ R4.7 — actor update; critic does TD(0) regression on the same delta

beats.text: With V of s at 5, V of s-prime at 6, reward 0.5 and gamma 0.9, the TD error works out to 0.9.

visual.label: TD error delta = r + gamma V(s') - V(s)

beats.text: REINFORCE has zero bias and maximum variance; one-step actor-critic trades a little bias for much less.
Consecutive samples from one rollout are highly correlated, which inflates gradient variance and destabilizes training.

visual.sub: Decorrelate by running actors in parallel

beats.text: A policy gradient must stay on-policy, so a replay buffer is off-limits; A3C instead runs many parallel actors.
The advantage actor-critic objective uses an n-step advantage plus an entropy bonus to keep exploring.

visual.note: EQ R4.8 — the template PPO and IMPALA build on

beats.text: The n-step advantage is the bias-variance compromise between REINFORCE at infinity and one-step at n equals one.

visual.note: n dials bias against variance explicitly

beats.text: A2C found that parallelism, not asynchrony, was the benefit; it batches lockstep environments into one update.
Vanilla policy gradients are step-size sensitive; trust regions and PPO's clipped objective made them robust.
